point_001: The paper lacks awareness of the broader 'transpilation' / source-to-source compilation literature, never uses the term, and makes questionable claims (e.g., that translation systems are less effective for code than natural language) that contradict well-known formally-verified transpilation work already deployed in production.
point_002: The approach is fundamentally bottlenecked on the availability of automated unit-test generation tools, which exist for only a limited set of languages and are largely absent for legacy languages (e.g., COBOL) — one of the stated motivating use cases.
point_003: The quality of automatically generated test cases is not analyzed. Tools like EvoSuite (and even better alternatives such as AthenaTest) produce incorrect or irrelevant tests a significant fraction of the time, and the paper does not discuss how noisy or incorrect tests affect translation model performance.
point_004: The strategy of translating Java-generated unit tests to Python and C++ via simple input/output templates and type mappings is underexplored and potentially fragile. It is unclear how the approach handles complex cases such as class instantiation, generics, dynamic typing, or constructs with no direct cross-language mapping.
point_005: The paper does not report what fraction of unit tests could be successfully translated across languages, nor does it characterize the types of tests that fail (e.g., those involving API call sequences, exception handling, or complex object construction).
point_006: The evaluation relies on comparing translated code against a reference implementation rather than against input/output pairs directly. As illustrated in Figure 4, correct translations can be marked wrong when the gold reference is itself an imperfect translation, and the prevalence of such incorrect references in the test set is not quantified.
point_007: It is not clearly stated whether the same data-filtering criteria used to select training functions (i.e., functions for which tests can be generated) also influence the composition of the evaluation set, which could introduce selection bias.
point_008: The paper does not analyze failure cases in depth: for example, whether EvoSuite disproportionately fails on longer or more complex methods, how multi-assert test cases are handled, or whether the model only succeeds on trivially short functions.
point_009: The necessity and cost-effectiveness of mutation scores for test-suite selection is not fully justified. It is unclear whether cheaper alternatives such as code coverage metrics would yield similar results, and the ablation on mutation scores does not conclusively demonstrate their added value.
point_010: The paper does not discuss whether unit tests themselves could be translated using the baseline neural models (TransCoder/DOBF) rather than heuristic scripts, and whether such an approach was considered or attempted.
point_011: Several references cite arXiv preprints for papers that have been published at peer-reviewed venues (e.g., ICLR 2018, ICLR 2015), and there are minor grammatical errors in the manuscript that should be corrected.
point_012: The collected GitHub dataset's licensing details are not provided; sharing the list of permissive licenses used would be valuable for reproducibility and for guiding future work on data collection from GitHub.
